mappings.rationaleAI: Sensory systems classified by receptor location (exteroceptors, interoceptors) and stimulus type (photoreceptors, chemoreceptors, mechanoreceptors) (B.1), with evolution of specialized organs (B.2) and sensory information processing (B.3).

The nervous system as the integrated signaling network covering nervous coordination (D.2), invertebrate nervous systems from diffuse to centralized (D.3), and vertebrate nervous systems including brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves (D.4).
The brain as the central organ of the vertebrate nervous system (D.4.a), with forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain components, and biodynamics of the vertebrate nervous system (D.4.e).
The eye as a photoreceptor organ (B.1.b) with convergent evolution across taxa representing the evolution of specialized sensory organs (B.2).
Hormones as endocrine signaling molecules (C), covering the relationship between endocrine and neural regulation (C.1) and vertebrate endocrine systems (C.2).
Sleep as a dormant state with lower metabolic rate (A.6), regulated by the circadian clock representing the biological clock and periodicity (E), with rhythms correlated to solar-day cycles (E.2).
The ear as a sound receptor and mechanoreceptor organ (B.1.b), with vestibular balance function, representing evolved specialized sensory organs (B.2).
Insects have endocrine systems relevant to invertebrate hormonal coordination (C.3) and centralized nervous systems (D.3) with compound eyes and antennae.
Arthropods have invertebrate endocrine systems including insects and crustaceans (C.3) and centralized nervous systems with ventral nerve cords and segmental ganglia (D.3).
Metabolism regulation connects to inactive states accompanied by lower metabolic rates (A.6), with allosteric enzymes and hormonal signals controlling metabolic processes.
Physiology of regulation and integration spans homeostasis (A), sensory reception (B), endocrine systems (C), and nervous systems (D).
